danger_signals.visual_form: Animated guitars, drums, saxophones, accordions, trombones with hostile behavior
danger_signals.meaning: Surreal danger — familiar objects transformed into threats in game world

danger_signals.visual_form: Bouncers, security guards, martial artists, ninjas rendered as distinct sprites
danger_signals.meaning: Direct opposition from people — corporate/venue security blocking progress

danger_signals.visual_form: Floating television screens, power cords, falling objects, steam blasts
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental danger — studio/commercial space has become hostile

danger_signals.visual_form: Black spiders, black cats, gelatinous cube (Zantar), 'six-legged Beatles'
danger_signals.meaning: Surreal/fantasy threats — nightmare logic replaces reality

danger_signals.visual_form: Missiles from clarinets, shooting bagpipes, disco balls, Yellow Submarines
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat posture — enemies engage from range

safety_signals.meaning: Health/worthiness recovery — cultural reference to Stan Mikita's (film location)

safety_signals.visual_form: P power-ups, 'Schwing' items, thumbs-up icons, hearts
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invulnerability or enhancement — brief safety windows

safety_signals.visual_form: Transition to donut-collection only space after stage completion
safety_signals.meaning: Safe harvest zone — no enemies, only rewards

safety_signals.visual_form: Playable instrument becomes offensive tool with amp upgrades
safety_signals.meaning: Empowerment through iconic object — Wayne's musical identity = combat capability

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Guitar weapon; karate kicks (learned from Cassandra); 4000-volt laser gun (Garth)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Public access TV show setting; Gasworks nightclub; music stores; suburban locations

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Security guards and bouncers as minions; studio guards
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Ben's studio; Ben's condo; commercial venues; TV production spaces

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Purple gelatinous cube (Zantar boss); surreal/fantasy aesthetic
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: All enemies in SNES version are surreal transformations of real objects
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Noah's Arcade entry point; dream/game logic spaces; four surrealist level themes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Recognizable locations: music store, gasworks venue, studio, condo, alleyway
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Realistic enemies (bouncers, guards, martial artists); plot-driven progression toward contract retrieval

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Game within game / Dream state (SNES narrative)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Surreal enemy design (sentient instruments, food monsters, disco hazards); fantasy suburb level; purple cube boss
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies follow game logic rather than physical realism; progression through four themed levels; rescue mission for captured Garth

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Instruments become enemies; musical objects dominate visual language
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies are instrument archetypes (string, wind, percussion) with corresponding attack patterns

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Stan Mikita's Donut Shop (level 2 SNES theme)
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Shrunken Wayne perspective; food and beverage items as threats; pastry/coffee aesthetic
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies include donut monsters, coffee cups, sugar cups; grease and steam as environmental hazards

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Disco/rock aesthetic; bar furniture, disco balls, Beatles references, rock imagery
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies include dancing boot objects, Beatles variants, submarines; human bouncers; gas jets as hazards

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dreamlike neighborhood; final confrontation space; Zantar showdown arena
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss encounter; narrative climax; Garth rescue objective

environmental_storytelling.example: NES version moves through narrative spaces: music store → gasworks → alleyway → studio → condo, each tied to plot (obtaining Excallibur, meeting Cassandra, getting contract, saving her)
Sentient instruments in music store levels signal artistic/creative domain; food enemies at donut shop signal comfort/vice space; disco balls and boots at Gasworks signal music venue culture
SNES version's Zantar arcade setup justifies why real-world locations become abstract: they're filtered through video game logic, signaling protagonist has lost reality anchor
'Six-legged Beatles,' Yellow Submarines, Barry Manilow records as enemies encode 1960s-70s cultural touchstones; communicates setting and target audience in-game

environmental_storytelling.technique: Time limit mechanic as pressure narrative
environmental_storytelling.example: NES version imposes stage time limits, communicating urgency of plot objectives (retrieve contract, save Cassandra before producer wins)
Post-stage donut collection levels signal safe recovery periods, providing narrative breathing room between combat encounters

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Engage Wayne directly; positioned in Gasworks and venue spaces; represent human opposition
Found at Ben's studio; block access to contract; represent corporate security apparatus
Attack on sight; use weapon-appropriate behaviors (saxophones shoot, guitars fall, drums pound); indicate surreal world state
Appears as objective in Gasworks and Ben's condo; provides Wayne with karate kick ability; narrative anchor for motivation
Captures Garth; held in final fantasy suburb level; represents ultimate game-world antagonist (distinct from real-world villain Ben)
NES: fights alongside Wayne with laser gun; SNES: kidnapped by Zantar, motivates rescue mission; dual-character mechanic signals partnership
Progresses through levels collecting items; learns karate from Cassandra; plays guitar weapon in SNES version; navigates both real and surreal spaces

notes: Wayne's World SNES uses surreal game-within-game framing to justify visual and behavioral shifts from realistic (NES) to abstract enemy design. The manifestation layer communicates: (1) Real-world power struggle (Ben vs. Wayne) through location and guard enemies, (2) Creative domain under threat through object-animation (instruments, food, disco items), (3) Rescue narrative through Cassandra/Garth's captured states. Enemies serve double duty as both mechanical obstacles and thematic indicators — what/where Wayne fights communicates story progression. Guitar weapon is primary world-state indicator: presence of amps/upgrades signals empowerment and artistic agency. Time limits and bonus safety spaces create pacing rhythm that mirrors film's comedic structure. The two game versions (NES realistic, SNES surreal) represent different manifestation philosophies for same IP.
